title: Chapter 11: Greg At The Door
content: The buzzer went off at 7:14 PM.

Not the friendly buzzer — the one that meant packages or Elif forgetting her keys or the delivery guy with the samosas that Elif had on auto-order every Wednesday. This was the *other* buzzer. The long, sustained, angry buzz of someone holding the button down like it owed them money.
Maya looked up from her canvas.

Chloe's ear twitched. She was on the couch, in her spot, in the sunbeam (which had shifted but she'd shifted with it, a slow golden migration across cushions). Her hum stuttered for half a second — a micro-interruption, the capybara equivalent of a flinch — then resumed. Steady. Warm.
The buzzer again. Longer this time. The kind of buzz that said *I know you're in there*.
"Who—" Maya started.

She Checked her phone. No text. No warning. No "hey, I'm in the neighborhood" polite fiction. Just the buzz. Insistent. Entitled. The sound of a man who had never once considered the possibility that a door might not open for him.

Maya knew that sound. She'd heard it before, in stories Chloe told over cheap wine and cheaper takeout, stories about Greg and the office and the way he'd call her cell at 10 PM and say "just checking in" in a voice that meant "why aren't you working." She'd heard it in the tension that lived in Chloe's shoulders — the *old* Chloe's shoulders, the human ones, the ones that had been knotted like rope for the entire two years Maya had known her.
She knew who it was before she looked at the intercom.
"Greg," she said.
Chloe's hum stopped.

Not faded. Not drifted. *Stopped.* Like someone pulled a plug. The quiet that replaced it was sharp and wrong, a missing tooth in the apartment's mouth. Chloe's body went still — not the relaxed stillness of napping, but the other kind. The old kind. The *freeze response* that Maya had read about in one of Elif's nursing textbooks: the body's way of becoming invisible when it can't run and can't fight.
*No*, Maya thought. *No, no, no. We don't do that anymore.*

But the body remembered. The capybara body — warm, calm, built for peace — still remembered what it felt like to be the thing that Greg bulldozed. The memory lived in the muscles. In the hum that had stopped.
Maya didn't buzz him in.
She pressed the intercom button. "Hello?"
"Maya. It's Greg. From Chloe's office. I need to see her. Now."

His voice came through the speaker tinny and compressed, but the urgency was intact. That particular Greg urgency — not worried, not scared, but *inconvenienced*. The urgency of a man who had been kept waiting and found it personally offensive.
"She's not available," Maya said.
"I've been calling her for three weeks. Her phone's dead. She's not answering emails. HR says she submitted a medical leave—"
"She's on medical leave. That means she's not available."
"I need to *see* her, Maya."
"She. Is. Not. Available."
Silence on the intercom. Then: "I'm coming up."
The speaker clicked off.

Maya's jaw tightened. She crossed to the door, locked the chain, and stood in front of it like a very small, very determined wall. She could hear the blood in her ears. She could feel her heartbeat in her fingertips. She was not going to let Greg into this apartment. This was *their* space. Chloe's space. The couch, the sunbeam, the hum — that was *theirs*, and Greg had no right to—
A different buzzer. The door downstairs.
Then: voices. A delivery person, probably, saying "yeah, second floor" and a man's voice saying "thanks so much, appreciate it, just need to — yeah, I'm expected."
The delivery person had let him in.

*Of course they had.* Greg had that voice. That golden, confident, I-belong-here voice. The voice that made strangers hold doors and cashiers smile and subordinates doubt their own memories. Greg probably charmed the delivery person in twelve seconds flat. Greg could charm anyone. That was the whole problem.
Footsteps on the stairs. Heavy. Fast. No hesitation. The sound of a man who had climbed four flights with purpose and was not slowing down.
Maya braced.
The knock came like a demand. Three sharp pounds, then three more, then a pause — the kind of pause that was itself a threat, a you-better-open-this-door-before-I-do-it-again pause.
"Chloe!" Greg's voice through the wood. "I know you're in there. I can hear — I know you're there."
Maya's hand was on the chain. Her other hand was on the edge of the door. She could feel the vibration of his knocking in the frame, in the wall, in the bones of the apartment.
She cracked the door. Just the chain. Just a slice of hallway light and Greg's face.
He looked *terrible*.

This was not the Greg that Chloe had described — the polished, pressed, white-smiled corporate machine. This Greg had bags under his eyes that had their own carry-on luggage. His tie was loose and crooked. His hair — that perfect, product-dominated architecture — was doing something it had clearly never been told it could do, which was falling apart. He had the specific exhausted pallor of someone who had been running on spite and caffeine for long enough that the spite was running out.
"Let me in," he said.
"Go away, Greg."
"I need to see Chloe. *Now.*"
"She's not—"
He pushed.

Maya stumbled back — she'd been bracing, but Greg was bigger and Greg didn't wait and Greg had clearly never been told "no" by a five-foot-four illustrator with paint on her shirt and murder in her eyes, and he was not adjusting well to the experience.

He was through the door. In the apartment. Looking around like he owned it, like he owned everything, like the world was a space he moved through and other people were furniture — and then he saw the couch.
He saw the capybara.
He *stopped*.
Ten seconds of silence.

Not the comfortable kind. Not the kind that Chloe made, warm and full and held. This was the silence of a man whose brain had encountered something it could not categorize. A glitch. A loading screen. A spinning beach ball of the soul.
His mouth opened. Nothing came out.
The apartment held its breath. Maya held her breath. Somewhere in the building, a pipe knocked twice and went quiet, as if it, too, was waiting.

Chloe was on the couch. A hundred and fifty pounds of golden-brown fur, round ears, dark mild eyes. Still as a stone in a stream. Her hum had not resumed. She was watching Greg with the particular, patient gaze of an animal that had nothing to prove and nowhere to be.
Greg's mouth was still open.
Then, very quietly, from somewhere deep in his chest, a voice that didn't sound like Greg at all:
"Chloe?"
She hummed.

Low. Warm. The full hum, the one with layers, the chord that Dave had recorded and Elif had charted and Maya had tried to pretend she didn't need. It came out of Chloe like breath, like it always did, without effort, without decision — just the sound her body made when it was at peace, and the sound filled the room, and it filled Greg.
His shoulders dropped.

Maya saw it happen — watched it happen, in real time, like time-lapse footage of a building settling after an earthquake. The architectural tension that held Greg upright, that kept his spine rigid and his jaw locked and his hands in fists at his sides — it went. Not all at once. In stages. First the shoulders, collapsing like someone had cut the wires that held them up. Then the jaw, unclenching with an almost audible click. Then the hands, opening, fingers spreading, the white-knuckle grip on nothing releasing into nothing.

His breathing slowed. Maya could see his chest — the way it had been moving fast and shallow when he came in, the way it lengthened now, deepened, as if his lungs had just remembered what they were for.
He sat down.

Right there on the edge of the coffee table, because his legs apparently weren't going to hold him anymore. He sat like a man who had been standing for a very long time and had just been told the war was over.

"I've been calling and calling," he said. His voice cracked on the second *calling*, a fracture in the corporate lacquer, and underneath it was something raw and tired and *human*. "I've been — her workload. I took it on. I had to. There was no one else. The Hendricks account, the quarterly, the — I've been doing *everything*, and—"

His hands were shaking. Maya could see them, resting on his knees, trembling like a tuning fork that had been struck too hard. Sleep-deprived. Running on empty. Running on the fumes of empty. He'd been covering Chloe's job and his own for three weeks and it had eaten him alive and here he was, sitting on a coffee table in a Greenpoint apartment, falling apart in front of an illustrator and a capybara.
"It's been — I can't —" He pressed the heels of his hands into his eyes. "I haven't slept. I can't sleep. Every time I close my eyes there's another email, another—"
He reached out.

It was instinct. Pure, animal instinct — the way a drowning person reaches for anything that floats. His hand moved toward the couch, toward Chloe, toward the source of the sound that was making his chest feel like it was being unclenched fist by fist.
His fingers touched her fur.
The effect was *devastating*.

Maya saw it hit him like a wave. His whole body — every fiber, every tendon, every overworked muscle that had been holding him together through will and spite and the deeply held belief that stopping was failure — *released*. The trapezius muscles, those two slabs of architectural tension that had been supporting the weight of Greg's entire unsustainable life, dissolved. His neck lengthened. His spine curved. He exhaled and the exhale kept going, a long shuddering breath that seemed to empty him completely, not just of air but of everything he'd been carrying — the emails, the late nights, the meetings that could have been emails, the performance reviews, the quarterly projections, the grinding relentless unyielding weight of *doing it all*.
Another breath. Quicker. Gasping.
"Oh my God," Greg whispered. "Oh my *God*."
And then he was crying.

Not the polished, performative kind of crying that Greg probably did in front of mirrors to appear relatable. This was ugly crying. The real kind. The kind that comes from somewhere so deep it doesn't have words, just sound — a raw, broken, gasping sob that shook his whole body and wouldn't stop. He was hunched over the coffee table, one hand still in Chloe's fur, tears running down his face, and the sound he was making was the sound of someone who had not let himself feel anything for so long that everything was coming out at once, a burst pipe after a hard freeze.
Chloe hummed.

She didn't move. She didn't flinch. She didn't do anything except what she always did — exist, breathe, produce the low warm chord that her body made without trying. But her hum shifted, slightly, almost imperceptibly, into something deeper. Something that Dave would have seen on the waveform as a strengthening of the sub-harmonic, the 38 Hz infrasound ramping up, the frequency that you couldn't hear but could feel, the one that told your nervous system: *you are safe, you can stop, you can stop now.*

Greg's sobs slowed. Not stopped — slowed. Like a storm moving offshore. His grip on her fur loosened, then tightened, then loosened again. His breathing found the hum and synced with it without trying, the way a roomful of clocks eventually ticks together.
Maya stood by the door, holding the chain, watching the last person on earth she'd expected to see cry in her living room cry in her living room because a capybara touched his soul.
She didn't know what to do with this.
She did not have a protocol for this.

She looked at Chloe. Chloe looked at her — one dark eye, calm and steady, holding no judgment and no surprise. As if this were the most natural thing in the world. As if reducing Greg to tears of relief were simply what Chloe did, simply what she *was*, as involuntary and essential as the hum itself.
*The capybara effect doesn't discriminate*, Maya thought. *It doesn't care who you are. It doesn't care what you've done. It just works.*

Greg's hand was still on Chloe's fur. His breathing was almost normal now. The shaking had stopped. His face was a wreck — red, wet, twisted with the aftermath of something enormous. But his shoulders were down. His jaw was slack. For the first time since he'd walked through the door, he looked like a person instead of a crisis.
"I didn't —" he started. Stopped. Swallowed. "I didn't know it was like this. I didn't know — *she* didn't — I never—"
*Felt this*, Maya finished for him. *You never felt this. Because you never let yourself.*
She didn't say it. She thought it, hard, in Greg's direction, in the same way she'd thought things at Chloe that she'd never say. The universe apparently wasn't taking requests.
Chloe hummed.

The apartment held them all — Maya against the door, Greg on the coffee table, Chloe on the couch. The sunbeam had moved again, and Greg was half in it now, and the light caught the damp tracks on his cheekbones, and the radiator clicked once, and outside Brooklyn did its endless murmur, and the hum went on and on and on, filling everything, asking nothing, giving everything, a sound that had always been there and would always be there, steady as a heartbeat, warm as a hand, constant as the fact that some things — the truest things — don't need to be earned.
Greg sat there for twenty-three minutes.
He didn't speak. He didn't move. He just sat, and breathed, and held on to the fur of a capybara who had once been his employee and was now, in some fundamental way, his cure.
When he finally stood up, he looked like a different person. Not better. Not fixed. But *unclenched*. Like a fist that had been closed for years and had finally, without deciding to, opened.
He looked at Maya. Looked at Chloe. Opened his mouth. Closed it.
"I—" he said.
"I know," Maya said.
He left.
The door closed behind him. His footsteps went down the stairs — slower now, heavier, but not urgent. Not angry. Just tired.
Maya locked the chain. Turned. Leaned against the door.
Chloe was still on the couch. Her hum was the same as it always was. The sunbeam was almost gone now, just a thin gold line across the cushions. Her eye caught Maya's.
*Okay?* The hum asked. Or maybe Maya was projecting.
"Okay," Maya said.
She went back to her canvas. Picked up her brush. Set it down again. Pressed her palms to her eyes for exactly three seconds.
Then she painted.
Greg never came back.
But the next morning, there was an email in Maya's inbox — rare, because Greg didn't email roommates, Greg emailed employees, and Maya was not on his payroll — and it said, in full:
*Chloe's leave is approved. Indefinite. Tell her I'm sorry. I don't know what for yet, but I'm sorry.*
Maya read it twice. Showed it to Chloe.
Chloe hummed.
It sounded like something between *thank you* and *I know*.
